## Conclusion

I will now go through my data and get an overall picture. The histogram for "previous programming experience" shows a varied background, including the majority of students having less than one month of programming experience. This implies that beginners may benefit from increased chances to practice without risking poor grades. The bar chart illustrating "where students acquired programming knowledge" indicates various prior experiance. From taking your first course at UNC to high schools and online learning platforms, this data confirms that students enter class with differing levels of coding experiance. Graphs illustrating degree paths show the vast majority of our students to be enrolled in BS, BA, and Minor programs in computer science. This shows students commitment to gaining advanced programming skills and again supports our need for added practice activities. Lastly, the lineplot depicting the  statistics for different YouTube lecture topics being watched/searched demonstrates that some lectures are more challenging to comprehend and may require additional reinforcement. 
We may conclude that we need to add more intermediate coding assignments for both beginners and for those learning hard-to-understand programming concepts such as recursion. 
Extensions and Recursions (Just a few)
1. Make these extra practice assignments "optional": By doing this, we allow students to pick and choose things they feel they need to work on. If they are strong in one subject, but want to practice on another subject, they should be able to do so.
2. Low stakes or completion based assignments: By making these extra practice assignments low stakes, students will feel pushed to try them even if they are wrong. Any extra practice helps, even if your learning what your doing wrong.
3. Auto-Graded assignments: If we are able to use the auto grader for these completion assignments, then the TAs will not even have to do a thing. By doing this, students are given the opportunity to learn more and more about a subject, while the TAs aren't stressed with extra work that they need to grade.
